documents.title: Headstage
documents.text: The KonteX X-Headstage™ series is a family of high-performance neural recording interfaces designed to provide stable, low-noise signal acquisition across a wide range of experimental configurations.

documents.title: Headstage
documents.text: Engineered for both head-fixed and freely moving animal studies, X-Headstages combine compact mechanical design, flexible connectivity, and scalable channel configurations to support modern electrophysiology research.

documents.title: Headstage
documents.text: The series is fully compatible with the XDAQ acquisition platform and supports integration with multiple probe technologies and adapter ecosystems.

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC
documents.text: E-series X-Headstage™ is designed to offer the best value while maintaining the flexibility of the X-Headstage™ that can be used with several off-the-shelf probe adapters. The advantage of adapter based approach can increase the utilization and longevity of headstage usage. If the probe facing connector breaks due to daily wear, it can be easily and cheaply replaced.

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC
documents.text: E-series X-Headstage™ is denoted by the Xe- followed by R for recording only headstage and SR for switchable stim-record headstage.

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC, Dimension
documents.text: Weight 1.5g

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC, Compatible Acquisition System
documents.text: The X-Headstage™ delivers optimal performance when paired with the XDAQ™ system. It is also adaptable for use with a range of open-source hardware and proprietary systems, although signal remap adapters may sometimes be required. Below is a list of known compatible systems;

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC, Compatible Acquisition System
documents.text: ditional unlisted platforms may also be capable of supporting the XeR:

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC, Compatible Acquisition System

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC, Compatible Acquisition System

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC, Compatible Acquisition System

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC, Motion Tracking
documents.text: All XR Headstages are equipped with a built-in analog 3-axis accelerometer as a standard feature. The accelerometer is sampled at the same rate as the headstage input channels, and its data are available through the AUX1, AUX2, and AUX3 channels in the recorded data stream. The analog 3-axis accelerometer is included with all XR Headstages and cannot be omitted.

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC, Motion Tracking
documents.text: For applications requiring active cable rotation compensation, an optional IMU Sensor is available. When used in conjunction with a KonteX Commutator, the IMU Sensor provides real-time rotational motion data that allows the Commutator to automatically rotate according to the animal's movement, minimizing cable twisting during freely moving experiments. The IMU Sensor is intended for Commutator-based motion compensation and is not part of the standard headstage configuration.

documents.title: XeR16-HDMI-ACC, Motion Tracking
documents.text: XSR Headstages does not support 3-axis accelerometer.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI
documents.text: The X3SR32 is a 32-channel stim-record X-Headstage™ developed by KONTEX, designed for simultaneous neural stimulation and recording in electrophysiological research. Based on two RHS2116 chips from Intan Technologies, the X3SR32 delivers reliable bidirectional data acquisition and output performance in a compact and robust form factor.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Dimension and weight
documents.text: Weight ~1.1g

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Armor
documents.text: Optional protective covers are available for each headstage, offering extra protection and anchoring. These covers, crafted from Polyoxymethylene (POM), are lightweight and resistant to chemicals. There are three standard armor types, with options for customization in shape and material to suit diverse experimental requirements. It's important to note that the size, weight, and shape of these armors will differ across various X-Headstage™ models.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, S Armor
documents.text: The S armors with ear loops around the side used to anchor headstage to the accessories such as electrode adapters.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, M Armor
documents.text: The M armors are designed to provide protection while maintaining minimal size.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, IP Armor
documents.text: The IP armors are designed to provide the most protection and fully encase the X-Headstage. The X-Headstage™ with the optional parylene coating has been tested to the IP68 standard.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Compatible Acquisition System
documents.text: The X3SR32 X-Headstage™ is designed for seamless integration with the KONTEX XDAQ™ system, ensuring synchronized stimulation and recording with high fidelity. In addition to the XDAQ™, the X3SR32 is also compatible with a range of open-source and proprietary acquisition systems, provided appropriate signal remapping or interfacing is used. Below is a list of confirmed compatible systems:

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Compatible Acquisition System
documents.text: Intan Recording Controller (512ch or 1024ch)

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Compatible Acquisition System
documents.text: Intan Stim-Record Controller

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Compatible Acquisition System
documents.text: Open-Ephys acquisition box

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Compatible Acquisition System
documents.text: NeuroNexus Smartbox

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, 32 Channels
documents.text: Enables concurrent recording and stimulation on up to 32 channels.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Dual RHS2116 Architecture
documents.text: Implements two RHS2116 integrated circuits, each supporting 16 channels, providing flexible and high-fidelity analog front-end performance.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Compact Design
documents.text: Engineered for minimal weight and size, optimizing for head-mounted or implant-based setups.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Connector
documents.text: Equipped exclusively with a micro-HDMI connector to interface with the XDAQ™ system.

documents.title: X3SR32-HDMI, Protective Cover Option
documents.text: Can be optionally configured with a protective cover for enhanced durability and anchoring support during in vivo experiments.

documents.text: E-series X-Headstage™ is designed to offer the best value while maintaining the flexibility of the X-Headstage™ that can be used with several off-the-shelf probe adapters. The advantage of adapter based approach can increase the utilization and longevity of headstage usage. If the probe facing connector breaks due to daily wear, it can be easily and cheaply replaced.

documents.title: X6R128-HDMI
documents.text: The X6R line is KonteX’s high-channel-count X-Headstage™, engineered for advanced neural recording applications requiring 128 channels or more. Despite its high channel density, the X6R maintains a compact form factor, approximately 10 mm x 10 mm and only a few millimeters thick, making it ideal for space-constrained experimental setups.

documents.title: X6R128-HDMI, Dimension and weight
documents.text: Weight ~0.9g

documents.title: X6R128-HDMI, Armor
documents.text: Optional protective covers are available for each headstage, offering extra protection and anchoring. These covers, crafted from Polyoxymethylene (POM), are lightweight and resistant to chemicals. There are three standard armor types, with options for customization in shape and material to suit diverse experimental requirements. It's important to note that the size, weight, and shape of these armors will differ across various X-Headstage™ models.

documents.title: X6R128-HDMI, S Armor
documents.text: The S armors with ear loops around the side used to anchor headstage to the accessories such as electrode adapters.

documents.title: X6R128-HDMI, M Armor
documents.text: The M armors are designed to provide protection while maintaining minimal size.

documents.title: X6R128-HDMI, IP Armor
documents.text: The IP armors are designed to provide the most protection and fully encase the X-Headstage. The X-Headstage™ with the optional parylene coating has been tested to the IP68 standard.

documents.title: System Adapter
documents.text: System Adapters are designed to enable seamless integration between KONTEX acquisition hardware and external systems, accessories, or signal interfaces. These adapters provide flexible connectivity options, allowing researchers to bridge different connector standards, expand system compatibility, and integrate additional devices into their experimental setups.

documents.title: System Adapter
documents.text: By simplifying signal routing and interface conversion, System Adapters help ensure reliable data transmission while maintaining compatibility across various headstages, probes, and acquisition platforms.

documents.title: SAdpt-XDAQ-Smartlink
documents.text: The XDAQ to Smartlink Adapter enables seamless integration of NeuroNexus Smartlink headstages directly with the XDAQ system. This adapter allows researchers to bypass intermediary commutators and connect Smartlink-compatible headstages via HDMI interface.

documents.title: SAdpt-XDAQ-OM12
documents.text: The XDAQ™ to Intan RHD Headstage Adapter is designed to interface the XDAQ acquisition system with Intan’s RHD headstages via SPI cables. This adapter provides a direct and reliable connection path, enabling precise recording control while maintaining compatibility with Intan’s standard hardware architecture.

documents.title: SAdpt-XDAQ-OM12, Usage Scenarios
documents.text: The SAdpt-XDAQ-OM12 adapter provides a direct and reliable interface between Intan RHD headstages and the XDAQ system. It can be used in experimental setups where stable high- channel-count recording is required, and it also supports extended configurations through optional SPI cables, allowing flexible positioning of the headstages relative to the acquisition system.

documents.title: SyncLink
documents.text: The primary purpose of the SyncLink is to enable precise time-step synchronization between multiple XDAQ systems. This is especially critical when performing simultaneous XR and XSR experiments on the same subject, which require the use of separate XDAQ units.

documents.title: SyncLink
documents.text: By synchronizing multiple XDAQs, the SyncLink ensures that data acquired from different headstage types are time-aligned, enabling accurate integration and analysis across systems.

documents.title: SyncLink, Dimension and Weight
documents.text: Weight 230g

documents.title: SyncLink, Device Overview
documents.text:
① Sync Ports (IN / OUT) :
Each SyncLink is equipped with Sync IN and Sync OUT ports.
SyncLinks are interconnected using standard Ethernet cables.
The IN / OUT configuration allows synchronization signals to be distributed across multiple SyncLinks in a daisy-chain.

documents.title: SyncLink, Device Overview
documents.text:
② Harp Port :
A 3.5 mm audio jack is provided for connection to the Harp Clock Synchronizer.
An adjacent status LED indicates the operational state of the Harp Port.

documents.title: SyncLink, Device Overview
documents.text:
③ Expansion Port :
The Expansion Port is used to connect a KonteX IO Expander, allowing extended I/O functionality as required by the experimental setup.

documents.title: SyncLink, Device Overview
documents.text:
④ XDAQ Expansion Ports :
EXP1/2 (HDMI): Connects the SyncLink to an XDAQ system.
These ports enable synchronization signal exchange between the SyncLink and connected XDAQ units.

documents.title: SyncLink, Device Overview
documents.text:
⑤ Ground Ports :
SYS GND (4 mm) — The digital ground shared by the internal electronic circuitry.
Chassis GND (4 mm) — Connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.
As a starting point, we recommend bonding SYS GND and Chassis GND together and using this common node to ground the Faraday cage shielding. Since environmental conditions can vary significantly, users should evaluate and adjust the grounding configuration to determine the most effective setup for their specific environment.

documents.title: SyncLink, LED Indicators
documents.text: The SyncLink is equipped with multiple LEDs to indicate power and synchronization status:

documents.title: SyncLink, LED Indicators
documents.text:
① Power LED :
Indicates that the SyncLink is powered on.

documents.title: SyncLink, LED Indicators
documents.text:
② S1 LED :
Indicates the status of Synchronization Channel 1.

documents.title: SyncLink, LED Indicators
documents.text:
③ S2 LED :
Indicates the status of Synchronization Channel 2.

documents.title: SyncLink, LED Indicators
documents.text:
④ Harp Port LED :
Indicates the connection and activity status of the Harp port.

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: 1. XDAQ and PC Setup

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: Connect one SyncLink to one XDAQ, and connect each XDAQ to its corresponding PC. Each SyncLink operates as the synchronization interface for its associated XDAQ–PC pair.

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: 2. Interconnecting Sync SyncLinks

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: To synchronize multiple XDAQ systems, connect the SyncLinks using standard Ethernet cables via the Sync Ports.

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: 3. Sync Port Configuration

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: On the SyncLink connected to the first XDAQ, connect the Ethernet cable to the Sync Port (OUT).

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: Connect the other end of the Ethernet cable to the Sync Port (IN) on the SyncLink connected to the second XDAQ.

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: 4. Expanding the Sync Chain

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: Additional SyncLinks can be connected in the same OUT-to-IN configuration to synchronize more XDAQ systems as required.

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: 5. System Verification

documents.title: SyncLink, Getting Started
documents.text: Power on all SyncLinks, XDAQ systems, and PCs. Verify proper synchronization using the status LEDs on each Sync Box before starting data acquisition.

documents.title: SAdpt-uHDMI-Smartlink
documents.text: The X-Headstage™ to Smartlink Adapter is a precision-engineered probe-side interface specifically designed to bridge KonteX RHD-based X-Headstage™ systems with the NeuroNexus hardware ecosystem.

documents.title: SAdpt-uHDMI-Smartlink
documents.text: By converting the native RHD signal output into the standardized Smartlink interface, this adapter enables the seamless integration of KonteX headstages into NeuroNexus Smartbox acquisition workflows.

documents.title: SAdpt-uHDMI-Smartlink
documents.text: Engineered to maintain strict compatibility with the RHD architecture, this adapter preserves precise channel mapping and high signal integrity, ensuring reliable data transmission when interfacing between the two platforms.

documents.title: SAdpt-Port-EXP
documents.text: The Port Expander is designed for researchers looking to maximize the number of animals monitored by the XDAQ system. It allows each HDMI headstage port in the XDAQ to support up to four animals with XR Headstages or up to two animals with XSR (stim-record) Headstages. Please refer to the XDAQ model and headstage compatibility table for more details.

documents.title: SAdpt-Port-EXP
documents.text: The Port Expander conditions the control signal and provides isolated and dedicated electrical systems to each connected headstage. As a result, the ground and reference nodes from each animal's headstage do not interfere with one another.

documents.title: SAdpt-Port-EXP, Cable and Commutator Compatibility
documents.text: The Port Expander is compatible with all KonteX cable and commutator solutions. For optimal performance, it is recommended to use the same cable configuration and commutator model for all connected headstages.

documents.text: The SAdpt-XDAQ-OM16 adapter provides a direct and reliable interface between Intan RHS headstages and the XDAQ system. It can be used in experimental setups where stable high- channel-count recording is required, and it also supports extended configurations through optional Stim SPI cables, allowing flexible positioning of the headstages relative to the acquisition system.

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64
documents.text: The SR64 Adapter is essential for the proper operation of the X3SR64 Headstage. It features active electronic circuitry that conditions and merges headstage communication, which typically requires two separate HDMI cables. Unlike the standard X3SR32 headstage, which uses LVDS signal lines that require double the number of signal pins, the SR64 Adapter converts LVDS signal pairs into single-ended CMOS signals.

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64
documents.text: This allows it to drive four RHS2116 chips, which make up the X3SR64 headstage, using a single KonteX HDMI cable. The adapter has been meticulously designed and tested to ensure the reliable performance of the X3SR64 Headstage.

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64, Dimension and Weight
documents.text: Weight 200g

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64, Getting Started
documents.text: 1. Connect two HDMI headstage ports from a compatible XDAQ to the input ports of the SR64 Adapter using the included KonteX 80cm HDMI cable. A longer cable, such as the KonteX 3M HDMI cable, can also be used.

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64, Getting Started
documents.text: 2. Ensure the Power LED is illuminated, which indicates that the XDAQ is providing sufficient power to the adapter.

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64, Getting Started
documents.text: 3. Connect the X3SR64 headstage to the headstage port of the SR64 Adapter. While the 80cm HDMI cable is recommended, testing has confirmed that the KonteX 3M HDMI cable can be used between the SR64 Adapter and the X3SR64 Headstage.

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64, Getting Started
documents.text: IMPORTANT:

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64, Getting Started
documents.text: XDAQ CORE models do not support SAdpt-SR64.

documents.title: SAdpt-SR64, Compatible commutator
documents.text: Currently, there are no off-the-shelf commutators compatible with the SR64 Adapter. Customers requiring a commutator solution for the X3SR64 Headstage can contact KonteX to request a custom motorized commutator with an integrated SR64 adapter.

documents.title: Instrument
documents.text: KONTEX Instruments are designed to support the development, validation, and troubleshooting of neural interface systems. These tools provide researchers and engineers with reliable platforms for signal simulation, connectivity verification, and system diagnostics, helping ensure stable performance in neural recording experiments.

documents.title: Instrument
documents.text: Our instrument lineup focuses on improving system reliability, simplifying testing procedures, and accelerating the development of neuroscience hardware setups.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer
documents.text: The Hardware Short Circuit Analyzer is a high-performance device engineered for efficient and precise short circuit testing, supporting up to 256-channel combinations. This analyzer is designed to identify and diagnose short circuits across large-scale circuits and complex electrode configurations, making it ideal for use in electronic design verification, fault troubleshooting, and quality control processes.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, Dimension And Weight
documents.text: Weight 261g

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, Status LED Indicators for Testing
documents.text: Install the driver before connecting the XDAQ to a PC. XDAQ drivers for Windows and Linux are available on our website: https://developer.kontex.io. For macOS, search for XDAQ in the App Store.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, Comprehensive Multi-Channel Testing
documents.text: The device supports up to 256 test channels, enabling extensive and customizable testing configurations. It can handle intricate circuit layouts and electrode arrays, making it an excellent tool for complex applications that require high channel count accuracy.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, Standalone and Remote Operation
documents.text: The analyzer offers flexible operational modes, functioning autonomously or under remote control via XDAQ or UART interfaces. This dual-mode functionality allows for easy integration into various automated systems and testing setups, accommodating both on-site and remote diagnostic needs.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, Broad Adapter Compatibility
documents.text: The device is fully compatible with KonteX's robust ecosystem of adapters, which simplifies interfacing with diverse electrode types. This flexibility ensures smooth connectivity with a wide range of components, increasing its versatility for different testing environments.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, Custom Adapter Options
documents.text: For specialized testing requirements, custom adapters can be requested to ensure seamless interfacing with non-standard or proprietary electrodes. This customization option is essential for users with unique testing setups or those working with custom hardware configurations.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, High Diagnostic Precision and Efficiency
documents.text: The analyzer is built to deliver rapid and accurate short circuit detection, significantly reducing the time and labor typically required for manual inspections. Its advanced circuitry and design allow it to perform reliable diagnostics, minimizing downtime in production and R&D environments.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, Scalable and Adaptable Design
documents.text: Designed to accommodate both small-scale tests and high-density circuit boards, the analyzer is suitable for use across various stages of electronic development, from prototyping to large-scale manufacturing.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, STEP 1. Connect to the Computer
documents.text: Plug the short-circuit tester's USB-C connector into your computer.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, STEP 2. Launch PUTTY and Connect
documents.text: Open the PUTTY interface on your computer. Locate your specific testing device in the list and confirm the connection. Once connected, you will be able to monitor test results through PUTTY.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, STEP 3. Prepare for Testing
documents.text: Connect the components you wish to test (such as microwires, silicon probes, or electronic components) to the short-circuit tester using the customized adapter board.

documents.title: Short Circuit Analyzer, STEP 4. Initiate the Test
documents.text: Press the test button on the short-circuit tester. During the test, the LED indicator on the tester will flash blue to indicate the testing process is underway.

documents.text: The Short Analyzer uses serial communication to perform tests and receive results. Use any serial communication software with a baud rate of 115200 to connect to the device. (We recommend PuTTY on Windows; ‘screen‘ or ‘minicom‘ for macOS and Linux.)

documents.title: Inst-BrainSim
documents.text: The BrainWave Simulator is a precision signal-generation platform designed to emulate neural activity for end-to-end validation and calibration of neural acquisition systems—from electrodes and headstages to acquisition platforms (e.g., XDAQ and others) and integrated experimental I/O setups. Its compact form factor, battery-powered operation, and dual-channel programmable outputs provide researchers with a reliable solution for bench-top testing and in-lab system verification—without the need

documents.title: Inst-BrainSim
documents.text: for live tissue samples or biological inputs.

documents.title: Inst-BrainSim
documents.text: As part of our continuous product enhancement strategy, the BrainWave Simulator has been refined to incorporate expanded signal source options, improved electrical integration, and streamlined control features. These improvements are evolutionary rather than disruptive, ensuring that all devices retain full compatibility with existing workflows while extending their applicability to more advanced experimental paradigms.

documents.title: Commutator
documents.text: KonteX offers both passive and motorized commutator designs to support diverse electrophysiological paradigms.Engineered for minimal breakaway torque and seamless multi-modal integration, our commutators deliver high performance and usability while providing exceptional value for advanced neuroscience research.

documents.title: Commutator
documents.text: Our newly redesigned non-motorized commutator delivers enhanced signal quality and low friction operation that are improved over the previous generation. It features integrated animal-side cable management for secure mounting and convenient cable storage. This versatile design supports both XR (recording only, up to 256 channels per cable) and XSR (stimulation-recording, up to 32 channels per cable) headstages, providing a streamlined solution for diverse experimental needs.

documents.title: Commutator
documents.text: The Commutator PRO is our most advanced and highly customizable model, featuring a built-in low-noise motor and two flexible torque sensing options: an animal-side gyroscope or an integrated Hall sensor. Its onboard microprocessor continuously and instantaneously detects and compensates for cable torque generated by animal movement, ensuring stable recordings and naturalistic behavior.

documents.title: Commutator PRO
documents.text: The Commutator Pro is a high-performance, motorized commutator designed to prevent tether tangling during neuroscience experiments, ensuring smooth and uninterrupted behavioral recordings. It can operate using external power or its integrated, rechargeable lithium battery, offering flexible deployment options. A built-in microprocessor, working in tandem with a rotary magnetic sensor and/ or an external gyroscopic sensor, actively manages cable orientation by automatically counteracting twists.

documents.title: Commutator PRO
documents.text: The system is highly configurable, supporting up to four Neuropixels ports alongside a conventional probe driven by either recording or stim-record headstages, and offers up to nine GPIO channels for user-defined applications. Its adaptable mounting and cable management system, complemented by an optional passthrough channel, enables seamless integration across a wide range of experimental setups—providing robust performance and maximum versatility.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Commutator Pro installation diagram
documents.text: Always ensure the Commutator is mounted level. Installing the Commutator Pro at an angle may lead to increased acoustic noise and accelerated mechanical wear.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 1: Powering the commutator
documents.text: The Commutator Pro can be powered via either the USB-C port or the DC jack. When using the USB-C port—particularly during remote operation under PC control—ensure a stable 5V, 1A power supply, compliant with USB 3.0 or USB Power Delivery (USB-PD) specifications. Powering the device with only 5V, 0.5A (typical of USB 2.0) may result in unreliable performance, including failure to power on or disruptions in motion tracking.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Battery charging
documents.text: If your Commutator Pro is equipped with an internal battery (models designated with -B), the battery will begin charging automatically when power is connected. During charging, the front-panel battery level indicator LEDs will flash and illuminate progressively to reflect the current charge level, indicating active charging (see LED Indicators section for details).

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Battery charging
documents.text: Once fully charged, all four power LEDs will remain steadily illuminated. The Commutator Pro is equipped with an integrated Battery Management System (BMS) that prevents overcharging, making it safe to leave the device plugged in during operation.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Battery charging
documents.text: A full charge takes approximately 7 hours, and under normal operating conditions, the device can run for up to 6-9 hours on a full charge.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 2: Connecting to the XDAQ
documents.text: Use the supplied DAQ cable to connect between a XDAQ headstage port and the appropriate headstage port (Neuropixels or HDMI) of the Commutator Pro.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 2: Connecting to the XDAQ
documents.text: IMPORTANT:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 2: Connecting to the XDAQ
documents.text: For Neuropixels, connect the black USB-C connector to the DAQ systems (XDAQ, PXIQBSC, OneBox) and the white connector to the Commutator Pro. Reversing the connector order will cause Neuropixels to be un-detectable.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 3: Connecting the headstage cable to the rotating interface
documents.text: To connect the headstage cable to the Commutator Pro, locate the headstage port(s) positioned on the underside of the device, which serves as the rotating interface of the commutator.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 3: Connecting the headstage cable to the rotating interface
documents.text: Identify the appropriate port based on the headstage type (HDMI or Neuropixels) as labeled near each connector. Carefully align the headstage cable connector with the corresponding port and insert it straight and firmly until it is securely seated. These ports are designed to maintain a stable connection while allowing continuous rotation during operation.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 3: Connecting the headstage cable to the rotating interface
documents.text: IMPORTANT:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 3: Connecting the headstage cable to the rotating interface
documents.text: - Ensure the cable is connected to the correct port.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 3: Connecting the headstage cable to the rotating interface
documents.text: - Avoid twisting or pulling the cable during installation.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 3: Connecting the headstage cable to the rotating interface
documents.text: - It is recommended to power off the device before making connections to prevent hardware damage.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 3: Connecting the headstage cable to the rotating interface
documents.text: Once connected, signal input from the headstage will be routed through the commutator and transmitted to the corresponding output port on the top of the commutator base.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 4: Managing excess cable
documents.text: When connecting the headstage cable to the Commutator Pro, the signal pathway begins at the rotating interface and exits through the fixed-side rotor side ports. After selecting the appropriate rotor side port (e.g., HDMI or Neuropixels), the user may route the cable toward the downstream recording amplifier.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 4: Managing excess cable
documents.text: To ensure stable cable handling during rotation and prevent signal artifacts caused by tension or displacement, proper cable management is essential.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 4: Managing excess cable
documents.text: Route the cable as illustrated below and securely lock the cable using the integrated screw clips, ensuring that adequate slack is maintained to prevent tension on the headstage connection and to allow smooth rotation of the commutator.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 4: Managing excess cable
documents.text: IMPORTANT:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 4: Managing excess cable
documents.text: It is important to leave a small amount of slack in the cable to prevent tension that could affect the device's movement and sensitivity.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 5: Sensor configuration
documents.text: The Commutator Pro can be driven using a compatible animal side IMU sensor or the integrated magnetic encoder. Note: the IMU sensor found in the X-Headstage or Intan stock recording headstages is not compatible with the Commutator Pro.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Sensor Models (designated with -I or -IH)
documents.text: Connect the integrated IMU sensor cable to the GYRO port on the rotating interface.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Sensor Models (designated with -I or -IH)
documents.text: -IH Model:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Sensor Models (designated with -I or -IH)
documents.text: Disconnect the integrated IMU sensor cable from the GYRO port, if applicable. Connect a custom headstage that includes a compatible and integrated IMU sensor to the HDMI cable.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Sensor Models (designated with -I or -IH)
documents.text: If no compatible headstage is available, connect the integrated IMU sensor cable instead. The device will not function correctly if both custom GYRO headstage and integrated IMU sensor cable are connected.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Magnetic sensor model (designated with -M)
documents.text: This configuration uses the integrated magnetic encoder located on the rotating interface to detect cable rotation. It eliminates the need for the external IMU sensor. No setup is necessary, proceed to the next step.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Combined GYRO & magnetic sensor model (designated with -MI)
documents.text: To use IMU sensor as the primary input:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Combined GYRO & magnetic sensor model (designated with -MI)
documents.text: If your Commutator Pro supports both GYRO and magnetic Sensor:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Combined GYRO & magnetic sensor model (designated with -MI)
documents.text: -IH Model:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Combined GYRO & magnetic sensor model (designated with -MI)
documents.text: Plug the integrated IMU sensor cable into the GYRO port on the rotating interface. When a valid GYRO signal is detected, the magnetic encoder input will be automatically ignored.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Combined GYRO & magnetic sensor model (designated with -MI)
documents.text: To switch to Magnetic encoder sensor:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Combined GYRO & magnetic sensor model (designated with -MI)
documents.text: Disconnect the IMU sensor cable. Release the lock screw securing the cable encoder attached to the magnetic encoder.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Combined GYRO & magnetic sensor model (designated with -MI)
documents.text: When using the IMU sensor, ensure the lock screw is fastened to secure the cable encoder in place.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Combined GYRO & magnetic sensor model (designated with -MI)
documents.text: IMPORTANT:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Combined GYRO & magnetic sensor model (designated with -MI)
documents.text: The IMU sensor must be connected to the Commutator PRO before powering on to ensure proper operation.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 6: Connecting GPIO
documents.text: This interface allows users to transmit analog or digital signals from external equipments. The signal enters via the fixed-side GPIO DB9 port and is routed directly to the rotating-side port, enabling seamless integration with devices mounted on the animal.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 6: Connecting GPIO
documents.text: A pre-assembled cable for accessing the rotating-side GPIO is available for purchase from Digi-Key or Mouser. To locate compatible options, search for 'JST SH' connector (10-pin).

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Step 6: Connecting GPIO
documents.text: GPIO signal can be routed through the HDMI port at the expense of reduction of maximum supported number of headstage channels. Contact us for the customization. Such customization should be done at the time of the order.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Attach IMU sensor to the headstage or cable
documents.text: Attach the IMU sensor to the headstage or the headstage cable using adhesive or other suitable methods.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Attach IMU sensor to the headstage or cable
documents.text: Ensure the sensor is perpendicular to the moving plane and parallel to the cable; do not rotate its orientation.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Power On
documents.text: Press and hold the power button for one second. The LED indicator will flash three times to confirm the device has successfully powered on.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Power On
documents.text: Press and hold the power button again for one second to power off the device.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Power On
documents.text: IMPORTANT:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Power On
documents.text: The IMU sensor must be connected to the Commutator PRO before powering on to ensure proper operation.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Magnetic Sensor Sensitivity & Speed Controls
documents.text: When using the KonteX Commutator Pro with magnetic sensor, two physical adjustment knobs labeled Sensitivity and Speed are available on the control panel. These knobs allow users to manually tune the system’s behavior based on the movement profile of the experimental subject.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ① Sensitivity knob
documents.text: Adjusts the magnetic sensor sensitivity (7 increments) of the Hall effect sensor.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ① Sensitivity knob
documents.text: Higher sensitivity increases the system’s responsiveness to small rotational movements of the tether, triggering sensor compensation with less motion input.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ① Sensitivity knob
documents.text: Usage scenario:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ① Sensitivity knob
documents.text: Small animals (e.g., mice) or low-activity experiments:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ① Sensitivity knob
documents.text: Turn the sensitivity knob clockwise to increase responsiveness.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ① Sensitivity knob
documents.text: Large animals or highly active behavior:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ① Sensitivity knob
documents.text: Reduce sensitivity to avoid overcompensation and motor jitter.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ② Speed knob
documents.text: Controls the rotation amplitude and velocity (7 increments) of the compensating motor in response to detected movement.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ② Speed knob
documents.text: Higher speed settings result in faster and larger motor responses, while lower settings make the compensation gentler and more gradual.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ② Speed knob
documents.text: Usage scenario:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ② Speed knob
documents.text: rapid or vigorous animal movements

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ② Speed knob
documents.text: , increase speed to prevent cable tension buildup.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ② Speed knob
documents.text: delicate behaviors

documents.title: Commutator PRO, ② Speed knob
documents.text: (e.g., head-fixed setups or fine-motor experiments), reduce speed for smoother operation.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Left & Right Manual Control
documents.text: The Commutator Pro control panel is equipped with left and right rotation buttons, allowing manual adjustment of the commutator’s rotating end.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Left & Right Manual Control
documents.text: These controls allow for accurate repositioning with each button press.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Modular Cable Fixation and Extension Components
documents.text: The Commutator Pro features a modular design for the lower cable fixation and extension components, allowing configuration changes by removing the mounting screws.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, This modularity applies differently depending on the commutator model :
documents.text: Except for Models with a Straight (COMP-IH-XH1-NP1-B)

documents.title: Commutator PRO, This modularity applies differently depending on the commutator model :
documents.text: Extension rod is removable and can be replaced with different lengths. The lower cable fixation assembly is secured with M3 screws and can be swapped to accommodate various cable types and experimental needs.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, This modularity applies differently depending on the commutator model :
documents.text: Passthrough Channel (-PT)

documents.title: Commutator PRO, This modularity applies differently depending on the commutator model :
documents.text: Extension rod is fixed and cannot be replaced. The lower cable fixation assembly remains removable with M3 screws for flexible cable management.

documents.text: For custom cable length configurations, please contact us to discuss customization and validation options.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Tested Cable Configuration
documents.text: To ensure optimal data transmission, minimize the total cable length between the DAQ, the commutator, and the animal. For custom cable length configurations, please contact us to discuss available options.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Firmware Interface and IMU Reference
documents.text: The Commutator provides real-time device telemetry and configuration control through a USB UART interface. Starting from firmware version 1.2, the system outputs IMU sensor data, motor position information, and system status using a JSON-based communication protocol.，

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Firmware Interface and IMU Reference
documents.text: This interface allows users to monitor device orientation, acceleration, and motor behavior during operation. It also enables configuration of operational parameters such as the Y-axis tilt threshold and telemetry output rate.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Firmware Interface and IMU Reference
documents.text: The IMU data reported by the firmware follows the coordinate system defined by the onboard Bosch BNO055 sensor.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Coordinate System
documents.text: The onboard IMU follows the coordinate system defined by the Bosch BNO055 orientation sensor. All orientation, acceleration, and gravity values reported in the telemetry output correspond to this reference frame.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Coordinate System
documents.text: In this coordinate system:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Coordinate System
documents.text: The X-axis represents rotation along the device width direction.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Coordinate System
documents.text: The Y-axis represents forward and backward tilt relative to the device.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Coordinate System
documents.text: The Z-axis represents vertical rotation perpendicular to the device surface.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, IMU Coordinate System
documents.text: These axes define the reference frame used for all IMU measurements.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Relationship Between IMU Data and Telemetry Parameters
documents.text: The telemetry parameters correspond directly to the IMU axes described above.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Orientation
documents.text: ox — rotation angle around the X-axis

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Orientation
documents.text: oy — rotation angle around the Y-axis

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Orientation
documents.text: oz — rotation angle around the Z-axis

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Acceleration
documents.text: gx, gy, gz — gravity vector components along X, Y, Z axes

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Acceleration
documents.text: These values describe the spatial orientation and movement of the commutator during operation.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Motor Response Based on IMU Tilt
documents.text: The Commutator uses the gravity component along the Y-axis (gy) to determine whether the motor should respond.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Motor Response Based on IMU Tilt
documents.text: During operation, the firmware continuously monitors the device tilt along the Y-axis. If the detected tilt exceeds the configured gy_range threshold, motor movement will be temporarily disabled.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Motor Response Based on IMU Tilt
documents.text: This mechanism prevents unstable motor operation when the device is excessively tilted and helps maintain reliable cable management during freely moving experiments.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Command Interface
documents.text: The firmware supports configuration commands sent through the USB UART interface using JSON format.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Command Interface
documents.text: Commands are applied immediately after they are received.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, gy_range Configuration
documents.text: The gy_range parameter defines the allowable tilt range along the Y-axis before motor movement is disabled.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, • Default Value
documents.text: gy_range = 5

documents.title: Commutator PRO, • Range
documents.text: A larger value requires the device to remain within a smaller tilt range before the motor is allowed to move.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, • Example command
documents.text: {"gy_range":6}

documents.title: Commutator PRO, • Example command
documents.text: This parameter is stored in non-volatile memory and remains unchanged after power cycling.

documents.text: The data rate parameter (dr) controls the telemetry output interval.

documents.text: This command sets the telemetry output interval to 500 ms.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, • Example command
documents.text: The data rate setting is not saved after power cycling and will return to 20 ms after reboot.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, • Example command

documents.title: Commutator PRO, • Example command

documents.title: Commutator PRO, System Verification and IMU Status Monitoring
documents.text: This section outlines the procedures for establishing a connection between the Commutator Pro and your workstation using CoolTerm, and how to verify the operational integrity of the integrated IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit).

documents.title: Commutator PRO, 1. Software Installation and Acquisition
documents.text: To facilitate communication with the Commutator Pro, users are required to utilize the CoolTerm serial terminal utility as the primary interface for system configuration and monitoring.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, 1. Software Installation and Acquisition
documents.text: Official Download Link: Users can access the software at

documents.title: Commutator PRO, 1. Software Installation and Acquisition

documents.text: Connect the Commutator Pro to your PC via USB and power on the device.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, 2. Connection Procedures

documents.title: Commutator PRO, 2. Connection Procedures

documents.title: Commutator PRO, 3. Interpreting the Startup Sequence
documents.text: The system performs a self-test upon connection. Use the following logs to verify the status of the IMU sensor.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, A. Normal Operational Status (Success)
documents.text: A successful connection is confirmed when the terminal displays the initialization sequence below:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, A. Normal Operational Status (Success)
documents.text: 1. Sensor Detection:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, A. Normal Operational Status (Success)
documents.text: The string BNO055 detected appears.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, A. Normal Operational Status (Success)
documents.text: 2. System Setup:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, A. Normal Operational Status (Success)
documents.text: The log will show IMU Mode 1, Low Power Mode, and the motor initialization (Setup Motor, Init Motor, Motor On).

documents.title: Commutator PRO, A. Normal Operational Status (Success)
documents.text: 3.Data Streaming:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, A. Normal Operational Status (Success)
documents.text: Numerical values will begin to scroll, indicating real-time orientation tracking is active.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, A. Normal Operational Status (Success)
documents.text: It is normal to see several .Failed to send! messages at the very top of the log during the initial handshake. These can be ignored once the BNO055 detected message appears.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, B. IMU Disconnected Status (Error)
documents.text: If the IMU sensor is disconnected or failing to communicate, the terminal will display a repetitive error log:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, B. IMU Disconnected Status (Error)
documents.text: Error Message:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, B. IMU Disconnected Status (Error)
documents.text: The screen will fill with Not detected and No BNO055 detected.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, B. IMU Disconnected Status (Error)
documents.text: System Stall:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, B. IMU Disconnected Status (Error)
documents.text: You will see .Failed to send! repeated, and the motor initialization sequence will not start.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, 4. Troubleshooting the IMU Link
documents.text: If a "Disconnected" state is observed, please perform the following diagnostic steps before proceeding with your experiment:

documents.text: 2. Reset Connection:

documents.title: Commutator PRO, 4. Troubleshooting the IMU Link
documents.text: Click Disconnect in CoolTerm and then Connect again to refresh the communication buffer.

documents.title: Commutator PRO, Mitigating EMI-Induced Errors in GMSL Differential Signal Routing Near Power-Switched Commutators
documents.text: The Commutator Pro is designed to provide reliable power switching in high-speed data transmission systems such as NP serial transmit applications. When operating with GMSL differential signals, users must take precautions to avoid electromagnetic interference (EMI) that may lead to errors during PRBS or serial transmission testing.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI
documents.text: The Standard Commutator HDMI is a passive, low-torque commutator designed for KonteX X-Headstage™ systems. It enables continuous mechanical rotation while maintaining stable electrical connections, minimizing cable twisting and mechanical stress during freely moving animal experiments. The commutator is compatible with XR headstages (up to 256 channels) and XSR headstages (up to 32 channels) using an HDMI-based interface.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI
documents.text: This commutator is intended for use in freely moving or rotating experimental setups, where continuous rotation is required without compromising signal continuity or introducing additional electrical noise.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Commutator STD Overview
documents.text: The commutator operates as a fully passive device, containing no active electronics or signal conditioning components.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Commutator STD Overview
documents.text: Signal transmission is maintained through internal rotating contacts, allowing unrestricted rotational motion while preserving channel integrity.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Commutator STD Overview
documents.text: The passive design ensures zero power consumption and eliminates potential noise sources associated with active circuitry.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Passive Signal Routing

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Fully passive design with no active electronics, ensuring transparent signal transmission without added noise or power requirements.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Low-Torque Continuous Rotation

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Smooth, low-resistance rotation minimizes cable twisting and mechanical stress during freely moving behavioral experiments.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: X-Headstage Compatibility

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Compatible with KonteX XR headstages (up to 256 channels) and XSR headstages (up to 32 channels) via an HDMI-based interface.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: HDMI High-Density Interface

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Standardized HDMI connectors provide reliable, high-density signal routing and ease of system integration.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Optimized Mechanical Design

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Designed for overhead mounting with durable construction to support repeated rotation and long-term experimental use.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Reduced Cable and Connector Wear

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Key Features
documents.text: Proper cable routing around the rotating shaft reduces strain, extending cable and connector lifespan.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Assembly Process
documents.text:
① Mounting the Commutator
Securely mount the Standard Commutator HDMI (Commutator STD) above the behavior box or experimental arena. Ensure the commutator is firmly fixed and aligned vertically to allow smooth, unrestricted rotation during operation.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Assembly Process
documents.text:
② Headstage-to-Commutator Connection
Using a compatible HDMI cable, connect the X-Headstage to the Commutator STD. Verify that the connectors are fully seated and properly aligned before proceeding.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Assembly Process
documents.text:
③ Cable Routing and Strain Management
Route the cable according to the illustrated configuration, wrapping and securing it around the rotating shaft of the Commutator STD as indicated. Leave sufficient cable length to accommodate the animal’s full range of motion while minimizing excess slack that could introduce mechanical drag or tangling.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Assembly Process
documents.text:
④ Final Headstage Connection
Connect the opposite end of the HDMI cable to the X-Headstage. Confirm that the connection is secure and that the cable routing does not impose axial or lateral forces on the headstage connector.

documents.title: Standard Commutator HDMI, Assembly Process
documents.text:
⑤ Commutator-to-XDAQ Connection
Connect the fixed (non-rotating) end of the Commutator STD to the XDAQ acquisition system using a compatible HDMI cable. Ensure the connection is secure and that the cable is properly strain-relieved.
NOTE:
1. Mount the commutator in a vertical orientation to ensure optimal rotational performance.
2. Avoid excessive axial or lateral forces on the HDMI connectors.
3. Ensure sufficient cable slack to allow free rotation without tension.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update
documents.text: This manual provides the step-by-step procedure to update the firmware on your Commutator Pro. Please ensure you have the correct

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update
documents.text: update file provided by KonteX before proceeding.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Preparation
documents.text: Ensure your Commutator Pro is powered off before starting the process.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Preparation
documents.text: Prepare a high-quality USB cable to connect the device to your PC.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Preparation
documents.text: A paperclip or a similar thin tool is required to access the firmware update button.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Preparation
documents.text: Locate the Firmware Update Button (pinhole) on the device interface.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Step-by-Step Update Procedure
documents.text: 1. Connect to PC: Plug the USB cable from the Commutator Pro's USB port into your computer.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Step-by-Step Update Procedure
documents.text: 2. Enter Bootloader Mode:

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Step-by-Step Update Procedure
documents.text: Use a thin tool (like a paperclip) to press and hold the pinhole button.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Step-by-Step Update Procedure
documents.text: While holding the button, switch the Power on.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Step-by-Step Update Procedure
documents.text: 3. Identify Device: Your PC will recognize the device as a new removable drive named "RPI-RP2". You can now release the pinhole button.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Step-by-Step Update Procedure
documents.text: 4. Deploy Firmware: Drag and drop (or copy) the provided .uf2 firmware file directly into the "RPI-RP2" drive.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Step-by-Step Update Procedure
documents.text: 5. Installation: Wait for the file transfer to complete. The drive will automatically disappear from your file explorer once the update is processed.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Step-by-Step Update Procedure
documents.text: 6. Finalize Update: Toggle the power switch to Restart the Commutator. Your device is now updated and ready for use.

documents.title: Commutator PRO Firmware Update, Step-by-Step Update Procedure
documents.text: Do not disconnect the USB cable or power off the device while the drive is active and the file is being transferred, as this may result in firmware corruption.

documents.text: Probe Adapters serve as modular interface bridges, ensuring correct signal routing, stable electrical connectivity, and consistent recording performance.

documents.title: Adpt-X6-EXT
documents.text: The X6 Cycle Extender (SKU: Adpt-X6-EXT) is a disposable interface adapter designed for use with X6 headstages. It provides a sacrificial connection point that helps reduce mechanical wear and tear on the X6 headstage connector from repeated mating cycles. This accessory prolongs the usable life of the expensive headstage connector by absorbing the connection cycles instead of the headstage itself.

By absorbing connector wear, the Cycle Extender helps prolong the operational lifespan of X3 headstages.

documents.title: Adpt-OM64
documents.text: The Adpt-Omnetics series is designed to interface KonteX X3R headstages with probes using a support 16-, 32-, and 64-channel Omnetics connector's probe. Available in three configurations— standard with screw-locking for secure attachment, no-lock for a reduced footprint, and vertical to provide additional clearance near the craniotomy—these adapters enable flexible integration into diverse experimental setups while maintaining a compact and reliable connection.

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2
documents.text: MEA (Microelectrode Array) is a simplified electrophysiological signal recording system designed to streamline the electrophysiological recording process. Its design allows users to conveniently swap different types of microelectrode array chips based on specific experimental requirements.

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2
documents.text: Record electrical signals from multiple sites within the culture.

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2
documents.text: Replicate the biophysical microenvironment found in vivo.

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2, MEA2 Overview
documents.text: MEA2 exploded view

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2, Device Overview
documents.text: In vitro experiments often struggle to replicate the cellular conditions found in living organisms. Primary cells cultured on rigid multi-electrode arrays (MEAs) exhibit significant differences from their in vivo counterparts, limiting the predictive value of such data. MEAs utilize glass multi-electrode arrays to recreate the mechanical and electrical environments of in vivo cells in a controlled setting. They can be paired with various headstages and stimulation array boards for optical

documents.title: Adpt-MEA2, Device Overview
documents.text: imaging and electrical stimulation/recording, effectively bridging the gap between in vitro and in vivo research and enhancing the relevance of experimental findings.

documents.text: The Adapter X6 to Dual X3 is designed to convert the 128-channel input interface of the X6 headstage into two independent 64-channel X3 input ports. This enables full compatibility with existing X3-series accessories, probes, EIBs, and adapter modules.

documents.title: Adpt-X6toX3
documents.text: The adapter preserves signal integrity through matched routing, ensuring that recording performance remains consistent with native X3 headstage configurations.

documents.title: XDAQ
documents.text: XDAQ delivers seamless, native integration with Neuropixels 1.0 and 2.0, supporting high-density recordings of up to 4 probes and 3,072 channels per system. Its high-speed Thunderbolt interface ensures powerful, low-latency performance for real-time analysis and closed-loop neuroscience applications.

documents.title: XDAQ
documents.text: Fully compatible with standard imec headstages and cables, XDAQ eliminates the need for QBSC cards and PXI enclosures—simplifying setup and reducing system complexity. The XDAQ AIO offers all the functionality of OneBox, while delivering expanded performance and enhanced flexibility for advanced research needs.

documents.title: XDAQ
documents.text: As the top-tier model in the XDAQ series, the AIO sets a new standard for versatility and capability in neuroscience instrumentation, offering robust native support for large-scale neural recording, optogenetic stimulation, and synchronized video acquisition. It supports up to 1024 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 128 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. It also delivers seamless compatibility with advanced

documents.title: XDAQ
documents.text: electrode technologies—including Neuropixels probes—with the ability to operate up to four probes simultaneously for high-density, multi-region neural interrogation.

documents.title: XDAQ
documents.text: The ONE+ model is a uniquely versatile platform that unifies multiple capabilities into a single device. It supports up to 1024 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 128 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. Additionally, the system includes native support for advanced electrode technologies, such as Neuropixels probes, with the capacity to operate up to four probes simultaneously.

documents.title: XDAQ
documents.text: The CORE2 model is a uniquely versatile platform that unifies multiple capabilities into a single device. It supports up to 512 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 32 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. Additionally, the system includes native support for advanced electrode technologies, such as Neuropixels probes, with the capacity to operate up to two probes simultaneously.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO
documents.text: The XDAQ™ product line offers a suite of acquisition solutions designed to address the evolving needs of modern neuroscience research. These systems combine flexibility and performance, enabling integrated, multimodal approaches to a wide range of experimental techniques.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO
documents.text: As the top-tier model in the XDAQ series, the AIO sets a new standard for versatility and capability in neuroscience instrumentation, offering robust native support for large-scale neural recording, optogenetic stimulation, and synchronized video acquisition. It supports up to 1024 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 128 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. It also delivers seamless compatibility with advanced

documents.title: XDAQ AIO
documents.text: electrode technologies—including Neuropixels probes—with the ability to operate up to four probes simultaneously for high-density, multi-region neural interrogation.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO
documents.text: With its high-bandwidth architecture, extensive I/O options, and precision timing synchronization, the AIO is purpose-built for real-time, closed-loop experiments, multimodal fusion, and large-scale parallel data acquisition across complex behavioral paradigms.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Setting Up The XDAQ™
documents.text: Connect the DC plug of the supplied power supply, into the power input port.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
① Analog BNC Ports
AIO features two pairs of wide range (±10V), general purpose 16-bit ADC (AI-01, AI-02) and DAC (AO-01, AO-02) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector for easy access. These ports operate on the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR Mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30kHz.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
② Digital BNC Ports
AIO features two pairs of digital input (DI-01, DI-02) and output (DO-01, DO-02) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector. The digital inputs support both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The signal level for the BNC digital output ports can be configured via software to either 3.3V or 5V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30 kHz.
⚠
IMPORTANT:
Do not connect ports to unintended signal type. Permanet damage may occour.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
③ Thor Vision USB Port
XDAQ AIO features a on-board hardware video processor capable of capturing up to four UVC camera streams simultaneously, or a single high-speed camera stream at up to 300 FPS. Each video frame is precisely time-stamped with the corresponding neural data acquisition timestamp.
By utilizing a dedicated hardware video encoding engine, the system ensures reliable, lossless frame capture—eliminating dropped or skipped frames commonly associated with software-based solutions.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
④ LED Driver Ports
XDAQ AIO features 4ch of onboard high-power light source drivers. Drive LED light sources from commercial vendors like Thorlab, Plexon, TDT etc.
Stimulus intensity and modulation can be controlled independently of the main neural acquisition.Each light source driver supports up to 1A of power output and modulation frequency from DC to 10kHz.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
⑤ DB25 IO Port
The DB25 connector provides six additional pairs of digital I/O channels. The digital inputs on the DB25 connector (DI-03 to DI-08) are compatible with both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The digital outputs on the DB25 connector (DO-03 to DO-08) are designed for 3.3V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). The connector also includes supplementary device signals for interfacing with external devices.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
⑥ Ground Ports
Sys GND —
the digital ground shared by the internal electronic circuitry.
Chassis GND —
connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.
As a starting point, we recommend bonding the two ground ports together and using this common node to ground the Faraday cage shielding. Since environmental conditions can vary significantly, users should test and adjust the grounding configuration to determine the most effective setup for their specific environment.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
⑦ Expansion Ports
The EXP1 and EXP2 ports connect to XDAQ™ accessories, such as the I/O Expander, to provide additional digital and analog I/O functionality.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
⑧ 3.5mm Audio Port
This 3.5mm stereo jack enables audio monitoring of two selected headstage amplifier channels, configured via software: Analog Out 1 (AO-01), which is routed to the left audio channel, and Analog Out 2 (AO-02), which is routed to the right audio channel.
⚠
IMPORTANT:
Do not connect ports to unintended signal type. Permanet damage may occour.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, For Record-Only (XR) or Stim-Record (XSR) Experiments
documents.text: 1. Connect the Type-D micro-HDMI connector of the stock KonteX HDMI cable to the X-Headstage™.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, For Record-Only (XR) or Stim-Record (XSR) Experiments
documents.text: 2. Connect the Type-A HDMI connector to one of the XDAQ™ Headstage Ports. Always use Port 1, as other ports may be unavailable depending on the license purchased with the XDAQ™.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, For Record-Only (XR) or Stim-Record (XSR) Experiments
documents.text: 3. Optionally, a commutator can be used between the headstage and the XDAQ™ Headstage Ports.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, For Record-Only (XR) or Stim-Record (XSR) Experiments
documents.text: 4. Plug the electrode into the headstage.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, For Neuropixels Experiments (NP Mode)
documents.text: 1. Connect the Omnetics connector of the Neuropixels cable to the headstage.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, For Neuropixels Experiments (NP Mode)
documents.text: 2. Connect the USB-C connector of the cable to one of the Neuropixels Ports (Port 1–4).

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, For Neuropixels Experiments (NP Mode)
documents.text: 3. Optionally, a commutator can be used between the Neuropixels headstage and the XDAQ™. Refer to the Commutator User Manual for more information.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, For Neuropixels Experiments (NP Mode)
documents.text: 4. Insert the Neuropixels probe into the headstage by connecting the flex cable of the Neuropixels probe to the headstage. Note: The metal pads on the flex cable should face downward when inserting it into the headstage. Flip the flex cable connector down to lock the probe in place.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, 1. Connecting to a PC
documents.text: Use the supplied Thunderbolt cable to connect the Computer Port on the XDAQ™ to a Thunderbolt port on the PC. The cable is identifiable by the lightning bolt symbol on its connector.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, 1. Connecting to a PC
documents.text: The XDAQ™ can also supply power to the connected computer: CORE2 can deliver up to 15 W, sufficient to power most laptops. The Peripheral Port supplies up to 15 W of power.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, 1. Connecting to a PC
documents.text: The Peripheral Port can also be used to connect a Thunderbolt accessory, such as a storage device, which will be accessible from the host PC.

documents.title: XDAQ AIO, Reference PC configuration
documents.text: While Thunderbolt and USB-C share the same physical connector, they are not interchangeable. Thunderbolt offers significantly higher data transfer rates and requires specific compatible cables. Retrofitting a PC with a Thunderbolt add-on card is generally impractical, as these cards are model-specific and often incompatible with other systems. To prevent compatibility issues, users should verify that their PC includes native Thunderbolt support.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2
documents.text: The XDAQ™ product line offers a suite of acquisition solutions designed to address the evolving needs of modern neuroscience research. These systems combine flexibility and performance, enabling integrated, multimodal approaches to a wide range of experimental techniques.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2
documents.text: The CORE2 model is a uniquely versatile platform that unifies multiple capabilities into a single device. It supports up to 512 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 32 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. Additionally, the system includes native support for advanced electrode technologies, such as Neuropixels probes, with the capacity to operate up to two probes simultaneously.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2
documents.text: With comprehensive onboard and expandable input/output (I/O) capabilities, the XDAQ™ seamlessly integrates with standard laboratory equipments, ensuring compatibility across diverse experimental setups.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
① Analog BNC Ports
CORE2 features a set of wide range (±10V), general purpose 16-bit ADC (AI-01) and DAC (AO-01) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector for easy access. These ports operate on the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR Mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30kHz.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
② Digital BNC Ports
CORE2 features a set of digital input (DI-01) and output (DO-01) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector. The digital inputs support both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The signal level for the BNC digital output ports can be configured via software to either 3.3V or 5V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30 kHz.
⚠
IMPORTANT:
Do not connect ports to unintended signal type. Permanet damage may occour.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
③ DB25 IO Port
The DB25 connector provides six additional pairs of digital I/O channels. The digital inputs on the DB25 connector (DI-03 to DI-08) are compatible with both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The digital outputs on the DB25 connector (DO-03 to DO-08) are designed for 3.3V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). The connector also includes supplementary device signals for interfacing with external devices.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
④ Ground Ports
Sys GND —
the digital ground shared by the internal electronic circuitry.
Chassis GND —
connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.
As a starting point, we recommend bonding the two ground ports together and using this common node to ground the Faraday cage shielding. Since environmental conditions can vary significantly, users should test and adjust the grounding configuration to determine the most effective setup for their specific environment.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
⑤ Expansion Ports
The EXP1 and EXP2 ports connect to XDAQ™ accessories, such as the I/O Expander, to provide additional digital and analog I/O functionality.

documents.title: XDAQ CORE2, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
⑥ 3.5mm Audio Port
This 3.5mm stereo jack enables audio monitoring of two selected headstage amplifier channels, configured via software: Analog Out 1 (AO-01), which is routed to the left audio channel, and Analog Out 2 (AO-02), which is routed to the right audio channel.
⚠
IMPORTANT:
Do not connect ports to unintended signal type. Permanet damage may occour.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+
documents.text: The XDAQ™ product line offers a suite of acquisition solutions designed to address the evolving needs of modern neuroscience research. These systems combine flexibility and performance, enabling integrated, multimodal approaches to a wide range of experimental techniques.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+
documents.text: The ONE+ model is a uniquely versatile platform that unifies multiple capabilities into a single device. It supports up to 1024 channels of high-resolution spike recordings and, in XSR mode, provides up to 128 channels that can be dynamically configured for recording or stimulation. Additionally, the system includes native support for advanced electrode technologies, such as Neuropixels probes, with the capacity to operate up to four probes simultaneously.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+
documents.text: With comprehensive onboard and expandable input/output (I/O) capabilities, the XDAQ™ seamlessly integrates with standard laboratory equipments, ensuring compatibility across diverse experimental setups.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+, Setting Up The XDAQ™
documents.text: Connect the DC plug of the supplied power supply, into the power input port.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
① Analog BNC Ports
ONE+ features two pairs of wide range (±10V), general purpose 16-bit ADC (AI-01, AI-02) and DAC (AO-01, AO-02) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector for easy access. These ports operate on the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR Mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30kHz.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
② Digital BNC Ports
ONE+ features two pairs of digital input (DI-01, DI-02) and output (DO-01, DO-02) channels, each equipped with a BNC connector. The digital inputs support both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The signal level for the BNC digital output ports can be configured via software to either 3.3V or 5V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). In NP mode, the sample rate is fixed at 30 kHz.
⚠
IMPORTANT:
Do not connect ports to unintended signal type. Permanet damage may occour.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
③ DB25 IO Port
The DB25 connector provides six additional pairs of digital I/O channels. The digital inputs on the DB25 connector (DI-03 to DI-08) are compatible with both 3.3V and 5V digital signals. The digital outputs on the DB25 connector (DO-03 to DO-08) are designed for 3.3V logic high. The digital I/Os operate at the same sample rate as the X-Headstage™ (XR, XSR mode). The connector also includes supplementary device signals for interfacing with external devices.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
④ Ground Ports
Sys GND —
the digital ground shared by the internal electronic circuitry.
Chassis GND —
connected to the metal enclosure, the outer shield of the HDMI cable, and earth ground via the power supply.
As a starting point, we recommend bonding the two ground ports together and using this common node to ground the Faraday cage shielding. Since environmental conditions can vary significantly, users should test and adjust the grounding configuration to determine the most effective setup for their specific environment.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
⑤ Expansion Ports
The EXP1 and EXP2 ports connect to XDAQ™ accessories, such as the I/O Expander, to provide additional digital and analog I/O functionality.

documents.title: XDAQ ONE+, Setting Up The IO Ports
documents.text:
⑥ 3.5mm Audio Port
This 3.5mm stereo jack enables audio monitoring of two selected headstage amplifier channels, configured via software: Analog Out 1 (AO-01), which is routed to the left audio channel, and Analog Out 2 (AO-02), which is routed to the right audio channel.
⚠
IMPORTANT:
Do not connect ports to unintended signal type. Permanet damage may occour.
